sections.header: Verses 1–6 — Abram's Faith Credited as Righteousness

notes.note: MacArthur: 'Do not be afraid, Abram. I am your shield; your very great reward.' God speaks after Abraham's refusal of Sodom's king's offer. The divine assurance addresses both the vulnerability created by military action and the financial cost of refusing the plunder. God himself replaces what Abraham refused.

MacArthur: God takes Abraham outside and says 'Look up at the sky and count the stars -- if indeed you can count them. So shall your offspring be.' Abraham believed the LORD, and he credited it to him as righteousness. This is the most theologically significant verse in the patriarch narratives -- Paul's foundation text for justification by faith (Rom 4:3; Gal 3:6).

heb.paragraph: "I am your shield (māgēn), your very great reward" (v.1) — God's self-presentation after the battle of ch.14 addresses Abram's implicit anxiety: with four defeated kings and their allies somewhere in the region, Abram has reason to fear reprisal. The shield metaphor is one of the OT's most common images for divine protection (Ps 3:3; 18:2; 28:7; 33:20). God is both the protector (māgēn) and the reward (śākār) — the relationship itself is the compensation, not any derived benefit.

"Abram believed the LORD, and he credited it to him as righteousness" (v.6) — the verse Paul quotes as the foundation of justification by faith (Rom 4:3; Gal 3:6). The word ʾĕmunāh (from ʾāman — to be firm, reliable, faithful) means more than intellectual assent: it is the orientation of one's whole being toward God as trustworthy. The hiphil form (heʾĕmîn) suggests causing oneself to stand firm in another — leaning one's full weight on the divine promise.

"He credited it to him as righteousness" (v.6) — ṣĕdāqāh is forensic, relational, and covenantal: the right standing of a party who has fulfilled covenant obligations. Here, remarkably, it is credited (ḥāšab — counted/imputed) to Abram not on the basis of performance but of faith. The theological revolution of this verse is that covenant standing can be received through trust before any law is given or any act performed — the basis of Paul's entire argument in Romans 3-5.

panels.ctx: Genesis 15:6 is arguably the most theologically significant verse in the entire Old Testament. Paul quotes it three times (Rom 4:3, 9, 22; Gal 3:6) to establish that justification has always been by faith, not works. This is not a NT innovation — it is the foundational principle of how God has always related to humanity. Abram's faith is the pattern; his righteousness is the prototype of what Paul calls "the righteousness of God."

cross.note: Paul's full exposition: Abraham was justified before circumcision (ch.15 precedes ch.17), proving that faith, not law or circumcision, is the basis of righteousness.
The Abrahamic faith-righteousness is the basis of Gentile justification: "those who are of faith are blessed along with Abraham, the man of faith."
Abraham's faith in the impossible promise is the model for faith as "the assurance of things hoped for."
James (using the same verse!) argues that Abraham's faith was "completed" by his works — not contradicting Paul but emphasizing genuine faith produces action.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The divine word comes to Abram in a vision: "Do not be afraid, Abram. I am your shield; your very great reward." Sarna notes this is the first vision formula in the Bible — "the word of the LORD came to Abram in a vision." The promise of divine protection ("shield") may respond to anxiety after the battle of chapter 14 — having made enemies of the eastern kings, Abram faces potential retaliation.

Ve'he'emin baYHWH vayyachshoveha lo tsedaqah — "and he believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness." Sarna: "This is one of the most theologically momentous verses in the entire Old Testament. Faith — trust in God's word — is credited as righteousness. The theological weight Paul places on this verse (Romans 4; Galatians 3) is already present in the Hebrew: Abram did not earn righteousness by works but received it through faith."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Abram's response to the divine promise is remarkably direct: "You have given me no children; so a servant in my household will be my heir." Alter: "Abram does not simply receive the promise with silent faith; he argues with God. The patriarch's response to divine address is frank, even bold — he names the problem that makes the promise seem empty. This kind of covenantal frankness characterises the patriarchal dialogues with God."

The verse is a narrative comment, not direct speech. Alter: "The narrator's statement — 'he believed' — is deceptively simple. The Hebrew word heemin (believed/trusted) has the root aman — the firmness, stability, reliability from which 'amen' derives. Abram's belief is not an emotion but a settled, stable trust in God's word against all visible evidence."

Romans 4 and this verse together are the foundation of Calvin's doctrine of justification by faith. Calvin: "Moses declares that Abram obtained righteousness by faith. Righteousness is not imputed to him because of his own merit, but because he embraced the grace of God by faith. Paul correctly deduces from this that righteousness is not of works but of faith alone." The doctrine of imputed righteousness is grounded in this single narrative statement.

Abram's complaint — "you have given me no children" — is not unbelief but a frank statement of the problem that makes the promise seem impossible. Calvin: "We see that Abram did not rashly pretend to believe more than he felt; but, just as he was perplexed about the manner of the fulfilment, so he honestly uttered what was pressing upon him." Honest wrestling with God's promises is not opposed to faith but is a form of it.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "credited it to him as righteousness" (wayyachshoveha lo tsedaqah) gives extensive treatment to the verb chashav (reckon, credit, account). The note observes this is an accounting term — God puts righteousness to Abram's account. The note connects to Paul's use in Romans 4:3,22 and Galatians 3:6 and to James 2:23, discussing how the three NT authors use the same verse for complementary purposes (Paul: faith not works; James: faith demonstrated in action).

The NET note on Abram's question discusses the covenant lawsuit form: Abram is pressing God to fulfil his stated promise, not expressing unbelief. The servant-of-the-house reference (Eliezer of Damascus) reflects an ancient Near Eastern practice where a servant could inherit from a childless master.

poi.name: Between the pieces
poi.coords: A ritual space, no specific location given
poi.text: The covenant ceremony uses the ancient vassal-treaty form: both parties pass between slaughtered animals, invoking the same fate if the covenant is broken. But only God (as fire and smoke) passes through. The place is sanctified by the presence of the divine, not by geography.

notes.note: MacArthur: The covenant ceremony uses the standard ancient Near Eastern ritual of cutting animals in half so that covenant parties walk between the pieces -- invoking a self-curse: 'may this happen to me if I break the covenant.' God initiates and commands the ceremony; Abraham prepares it but does not yet pass through.

MacArthur: Abraham drove away the birds of prey from the carcasses -- a detail that signals his watchful attention during the covenant preparation. The birds of prey attempting to consume the sacrifices may symbolise the hostile forces that will threaten the covenant promise during the coming centuries of Egyptian bondage (vv.13-16).

heb.paragraph: "How can I know that I will gain possession (ʾîrāšennāh) of it?" (v.8) — Abram's question about the land uses the verb yāraš (to inherit/dispossess), which will become the technical term for Israel's conquest of Canaan (Num 33:53; Deut 1:8). Abram is asking for a legal guarantee of what has been promised verbally. God's response is not rebuke but a formal covenant ceremony — the divine commitment is made with the solemnity of a binding legal rite.

heb.gloss: to cut in two / to halve
heb.paragraph: The covenant ceremony of vv.9-11 involves cutting animals in two (bāthar) — the standard ancient Near Eastern covenant-ratification rite, from which the Hebrew idiom "to cut a covenant" (kārat bĕrît) derives. The contracting parties would walk between the divided carcasses, symbolically saying: "If I break this covenant, may what happened to these animals happen to me." The remarkable element in v.17 is that only God (as the smoking firepot and blazing torch) passes between the pieces — making it an unconditional divine commitment.

"Then birds of prey came down on the carcasses, but Abram drove them away" (v.11). The ʿayiṭ (birds of prey / vultures) descending on the covenant sacrifice are understood by some as threats to the covenant — nations and forces that will attempt to devour it — which Abram actively repels. His role as guardian of the covenant ceremony anticipates Israel's ongoing struggle to maintain covenant integrity against external and internal threats that seek to "pick at" the divine promises.

panels.hist: The covenant ceremony of Genesis 15 has direct parallels in ANE suzerainty treaties. In Mari texts and Hittite treaty documents, parties to a covenant would walk between the halves of slain animals, invoking the curse: "May what happened to these animals happen to the one who breaks this covenant." The stunning innovation in Genesis 15 is that GOD alone passes through — taking the covenant curse upon himself, making it an unconditional divine commitment.
panels.ctx: The one-sided nature of this covenant is theologically explosive. Abram is put into a "deep sleep" — he cannot pass through the pieces. Only the divine presence (fire pot and torch) passes. This means the covenant's fulfillment depends entirely on God, not on Abram's faithfulness. It is an unconditional promise, a suzerain's self-imposed oath. Theologians see this as the basis for the "new covenant" of Hebrews, where God's own Son bears the covenant curse on the cross.

cross.note: When Israelites "cut a covenant" and then broke it by re-enslaving freed servants, God invokes the curse of walking between the pieces — exactly the Genesis 15 ceremony.
Paul argues the Mosaic law cannot annul the Abraham covenant, which was confirmed 430 years earlier — an unconditional promise cannot be made conditional later.
God swore by himself because there was no greater (v.13) — the basis of "two unchangeable things" (promise + oath) giving "strong encouragement" to those who flee to Christ.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The covenant ceremony of the animal halves is an ancient Near Eastern ratification ritual — a self-maledictory oath in which the parties pass between the divided animals, invoking the fate of the animals on themselves if they break the covenant. Sarna: "Archaeological evidence from Mari and other sites confirms that the 'cutting' of animals in covenant ceremonies was a standard ancient Near Eastern practice. The Hebrew idiom for making a covenant — karat berith (cut a covenant) — reflects this practice."

The deep sleep (tardemah) that falls on Abram echoes Adam's deep sleep in 2:21. Sarna notes both accompany a divine creative act: in ch.2, the creation of woman; here, the creation of the covenant. The human is passive while the divine acts — the covenant's unilateral character is dramatised in Abram's unconsciousness.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Abram asks "how can I know that I will gain possession of it?" — the same frank questioning as v.2. Alter: "Abram wants a sign, a confirmation of the promise. God provides one — but the sign is entirely divine: a ritual that Abram prepares but does not participate in (he falls into a deep sleep). The covenant is confirmed unilaterally. Abram's question receives an answer that removes him from the ratification process entirely."

The smoking firepot and blazing torch pass between the pieces without Abram passing through. Alter notes the asymmetry: in normal covenant ceremonies both parties walked between the pieces. Here only God walks through — the covenant is entirely his undertaking, not a bilateral agreement.

The preparation of the covenant animals is an act of obedience — Abram follows the divine instruction precisely. Calvin: "Abram did not require a further sign because he was destitute of faith, but that he might more certainly confirm it to himself." The request for a sign is not unbelief but the desire for greater assurance — which God graciously provides.

Calvin: The division of animals was a ceremony used among ancient nations when forming a league — both parties passed between the pieces. God condescends to make his covenant in this human form, showing that he accommodates himself to our capacity.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the covenant ceremony explains the ancient ritual of cutting animals and passing between the halves. The note cites Jeremiah 34:18-20, where the same ritual is described and its self-maledictory meaning is explicit: those who pass between the halves are invoking the same fate on themselves if they break the covenant. The note observes that in Genesis 15, only God passes through — the covenant is entirely unilateral.

The NET note on the covenant-of-the-pieces traces its parallel in Jeremiah 34:18-19, where breaking the covenant is described as being like the cut animals. The oath-curse: if you break this covenant, may you be divided like these animals.

sections.header: Verses 12–21 — The Covenant Ratified; the Prophecy of Egypt

poi.name: Land of Promise: Nile to Euphrates
poi.text: The promised land is defined as stretching from "the river of Egypt" (Wadi el-Arish or the Nile) to the Euphrates. This is the maximum extent — never fully realized in any historical period but briefly approximated under Solomon. The promise is larger than any fulfilment to date.

tl.name: Covenant at the pieces — this passage
tl.text: God's prophecy: the seed will sojourn 400 years in a land not their own. Paul (Gal 3:17) counts 430 years from this covenant to Sinai. The covenant clock starts here.

notes.note: MacArthur: In a deep sleep, Abraham receives a prophetic vision of the 400-year Egyptian bondage. The precision of the prophecy -- nation, slavery, affliction, judgment on the oppressor, fourth generation return -- establishes God's sovereign foreknowledge of the entire redemption history before it occurs.

MacArthur: God alone passes between the pieces as 'a smoking firepot with a blazing torch' -- divine symbols throughout the OT (Ex 13:21; 19:18). The covenant is unconditional and unilateral: God incurs the self-curse, not Abraham. On that day the LORD made a covenant with Abram -- the formal covenant ratification is now complete.

heb.paragraph: A "thick and dreadful darkness came over him" (v.12) — the same deep sleep (tardēmāh) that fell on Adam before the formation of Eve (2:21), a state of prophetic receptivity to divine revelation. The combination of sleep and dread (ʾêmāh) marks the encounter as a theophanic experience of the holy — God's presence is not casually encountered but experienced as awesome and overwhelming. The covenant ratification will occur at this threshold between human consciousness and divine initiative.

The prophecy of v.13 — "your descendants will be strangers in a country not their own, and they will be enslaved and mistreated four hundred years" — uses three keywords that describe Egypt: gēr (alien/sojourner — cf. Exod 22:21), ʿābad (to serve/slave — cf. Exod 1:13), ʿinnāh (to oppress/afflict — cf. Exod 1:11-12). The Exodus is not an unplanned rescue but the fulfilment of a precise covenant prophecy given to Abraham four centuries earlier.

"The sin of the Amorites has not yet reached its full measure" (v.16) — the delay in the land's inheritance is theologically motivated: God will not dispossess the Canaanites until their covenant-breaking and moral corruption has reached a threshold that justifies displacement. The divine patience toward the Amorites for four generations is an act of mercy. When Israel eventually takes the land, it is not conquest for conquest's sake but the execution of a covenant that has been patient and just.

panels.hist: The covenant ceremony of Genesis 15 has direct parallels in ANE suzerainty treaties. In Mari texts and Hittite treaty documents, parties to a covenant would walk between the halves of slain animals, invoking the curse: "May what happened to these animals happen to the one who breaks this covenant." The stunning innovation in Genesis 15 is that GOD alone passes through — taking the covenant curse upon himself, making it an unconditional divine commitment.
panels.ctx: The one-sided nature of this covenant is theologically explosive. Abram is put into a "deep sleep" — he cannot pass through the pieces. Only the divine presence (fire pot and torch) passes. This means the covenant's fulfillment depends entirely on God, not on Abram's faithfulness. It is an unconditional promise, a suzerain's self-imposed oath. Theologians see this as the basis for the "new covenant" of Hebrews, where God's own Son bears the covenant curse on the cross.

cross.note: When Israelites "cut a covenant" and then broke it by re-enslaving freed servants, God invokes the curse of walking between the pieces — exactly the Genesis 15 ceremony.
Paul argues the Mosaic law cannot annul the Abraham covenant, which was confirmed 430 years earlier — an unconditional promise cannot be made conditional later.
God swore by himself because there was no greater (v.13) — the basis of "two unchangeable things" (promise + oath) giving "strong encouragement" to those who flee to Christ.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The deep sleep (tardemah) that falls on Abram during the covenant ratification is the same word used for Adam's sleep in 2:21 — a divinely induced unconsciousness during a divine act. Sarna: "Abram is passive during the covenant's ratification — he watches, but does not participate. The smoking firepot and blazing torch (symbols of the divine presence — cf. Exod 3:2; 13:21) pass between the animal pieces alone. God alone takes the covenant oath."

The land promise is now specified with territorial boundaries: "from the Wadi of Egypt to the great river, the Euphrates." Sarna notes this is the most expansive description of the promised land in the entire Abraham narrative. The list of peoples occupying the land (ten nations) is the most comprehensive in the Bible — emphasising that the fulfilment is not imminent but will require divine displacement of established peoples.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The smoking firepot and blazing torch passing between the animal pieces is the climax of the covenant ceremony. Alter: "The divine presence manifests as fire — not to consume but to covenant. The fire moves between the pieces that Abram has prepared. God passes through the self-maledictory oath alone, binding himself unilaterally to the fulfilment of his promise. Abram, asleep and terrified, is present but passive. This is covenant entirely from the divine side."

The prediction of 400 years of slavery in Egypt within the covenant ratification is striking. Alter: "The promise of land comes wrapped in the prediction of dispossession — before the land can be possessed, the people will be enslaved elsewhere. The covenant looks four centuries forward and tells Abram that the fulfilment will come through suffering, not around it."

The deep sleep and the "thick and dreadful darkness" that settles on Abram are appropriate responses to the divine presence. Calvin: "Moses here describes the circumstances of the vision, to inform us that Abram was fully awakened, and that there was no delusion about it. The terror and darkness indicate the gravity of what is being communicated — the people of God will suffer greatly before the promise is fulfilled."

The divine fire passing between the covenant pieces is God swearing by himself. Calvin: "God here binds himself, as it were, by a solemn oath, to Abraham's posterity. He did not merely promise, but added an oath — and that oath was not by another but by his own being. The smoking firepot and blazing torch are the divine presence, enacting the covenant."

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on tardemah (deep sleep) connects to 2:21 (Adam's sleep) and 1 Samuel 26:12, where the word describes a divinely induced unconsciousness. The note observes that Abram's passive state during the covenant ratification is theologically significant: the covenant is enacted by God alone while Abram sleeps. This makes the Abrahamic covenant a grant covenant — not contingent on human faithfulness.

The NET note on the smoking firepot and blazing torch identifies these as theophanic symbols of the divine presence, comparing Exodus 3:2 (burning bush), 13:21 (pillar of fire), and 19:18 (Sinai's smoke). The note emphasises the exclusively divine passage between the animal pieces — establishing the Abrahamic covenant as unconditional and unilateral.

ppl.name: Abram
ppl.text: His faith in the face of an impossible promise — childless at advanced age — becomes the paradigmatic act of faith in the Bible. Paul calls him "the father of all who believe" (Rom 4:11).

ppl.name: Eliezer of Damascus
ppl.text: Mentioned only here in the Bible. As Abram's chief servant and potential heir under the custom of adoption-for-inheritance (attested in Nuzi texts), his existence represents the human "solution" that God overrules.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Abram believed the LORD, and he credited it to him as righteousness."ESV"And he believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness."KJV"And he believed in the LORD; and he counted it to him for righteousness."NASB"Then he believed in the LORD; and He credited it to him as righteousness."NLT"And Abram believed the LORD, and the LORD counted him as righteous because of his faith."MSG"And he believed! Believed God! God declared him 'Set-Right-with-God.'"

src.title: Nuzi Tablets (15th c. BC)
src.quote: Cuneiform tablets from Nuzi (northeastern Iraq) document the custom of adopting a servant as heir when a man had no son — exactly Abram's situation with Eliezer. The tablets show that a subsequently born natural son would supersede the adopted heir.
src.note: Provides direct cultural background for Abram's anxiety about Eliezer and the legal framework God's promise overrides.

src.title: LXX (Septuagint) Genesis 15:6
src.quote: The Greek translation renders "counted/reckoned" (ḥāšaḇ) as elogisthē — the same word Paul uses throughout Romans 4. This linguistic bridge directly enabled Paul's theological argument about imputed righteousness.
src.note: The LXX translation choice becomes a key link in the NT's theological use of this verse.

rec.text: Luther called Genesis 15:6 "the foremost passage in the entire Old Testament" and the foundation of his reformation doctrine of justification sola fide. He argued it proved that righteousness before God has always been by faith alone, not merit.

rec.who: N.T. Wright, Paul and the Faithfulness of God
rec.text: Wright nuances the traditional reading: Abraham's faithfulness (as well as God's) is in view — the verse is about covenant loyalty, not merely individual forensic justification. His reading sparks the "New Perspective on Paul" debate, one of the most significant theological controversies of the late 20th century.

rows.label: Faith Credited as Righteousness
rows.text: One of the OT's most theologically concentrated sentences. Paul (Rom 4) and James (2:23) both expound this verse -- the ground of justification by faith.

rows.label: The Covenant of the Pieces: God Alone Passes Through
rows.text: Only God passes between the animal halves. The covenant's asymmetry is structural: God alone incurs the self-curse of the ceremony. The covenant is not bilateral but unilateral -- God binds himself; Abram sleeps.

hebtext.note: "I am your shield (māgēn), your very great reward" (v.1) — God's self-presentation after the battle of ch.14 addresses Abram's implicit anxiety: with four defeated kings and their allies somewhere in the region, Abram has reason to fear reprisal. The shield metaphor is one of the OT's most common images for divine protection (Ps 3:3; 18:2; 28:7; 33:20). God is both the protector (māgēn) and the reward (śākār) — the relationship itself is the compensation, not any derived benefit.

"Abram believed the LORD, and he credited it to him as righteousness" (v.6) — the verse Paul quotes as the foundation of justification by faith (Rom 4:3; Gal 3:6). The word ʾĕmunāh (from ʾāman — to be firm, reliable, faithful) means more than intellectual assent: it is the orientation of one's whole being toward God as trustworthy. The hiphil form (heʾĕmîn) suggests causing oneself to stand firm in another — leaning one's full weight on the divine promise.

"He credited it to him as righteousness" (v.6) — ṣĕdāqāh is forensic, relational, and covenantal: the right standing of a party who has fulfilled covenant obligations. Here, remarkably, it is credited (ḥāšab — counted/imputed) to Abram not on the basis of performance but of faith. The theological revolution of this verse is that covenant standing can be received through trust before any law is given or any act performed — the basis of Paul's entire argument in Romans 3-5.

"How can I know that I will gain possession (ʾîrāšennāh) of it?" (v.8) — Abram's question about the land uses the verb yāraš (to inherit/dispossess), which will become the technical term for Israel's conquest of Canaan (Num 33:53; Deut 1:8). Abram is asking for a legal guarantee of what has been promised verbally. God's response is not rebuke but a formal covenant ceremony — the divine commitment is made with the solemnity of a binding legal rite.

hebtext.note: The covenant ceremony of vv.9-11 involves cutting animals in two (bāthar) — the standard ancient Near Eastern covenant-ratification rite, from which the Hebrew idiom "to cut a covenant" (kārat bĕrît) derives. The contracting parties would walk between the divided carcasses, symbolically saying: "If I break this covenant, may what happened to these animals happen to me." The remarkable element in v.17 is that only God (as the smoking firepot and blazing torch) passes between the pieces — making it an unconditional divine commitment.

"Then birds of prey came down on the carcasses, but Abram drove them away" (v.11). The ʿayiṭ (birds of prey / vultures) descending on the covenant sacrifice are understood by some as threats to the covenant — nations and forces that will attempt to devour it — which Abram actively repels. His role as guardian of the covenant ceremony anticipates Israel's ongoing struggle to maintain covenant integrity against external and internal threats that seek to "pick at" the divine promises.

A "thick and dreadful darkness came over him" (v.12) — the same deep sleep (tardēmāh) that fell on Adam before the formation of Eve (2:21), a state of prophetic receptivity to divine revelation. The combination of sleep and dread (ʾêmāh) marks the encounter as a theophanic experience of the holy — God's presence is not casually encountered but experienced as awesome and overwhelming. The covenant ratification will occur at this threshold between human consciousness and divine initiative.

The prophecy of v.13 — "your descendants will be strangers in a country not their own, and they will be enslaved and mistreated four hundred years" — uses three keywords that describe Egypt: gēr (alien/sojourner — cf. Exod 22:21), ʿābad (to serve/slave — cf. Exod 1:13), ʿinnāh (to oppress/afflict — cf. Exod 1:11-12). The Exodus is not an unplanned rescue but the fulfilment of a precise covenant prophecy given to Abraham four centuries earlier.

"The sin of the Amorites has not yet reached its full measure" (v.16) — the delay in the land's inheritance is theologically motivated: God will not dispossess the Canaanites until their covenant-breaking and moral corruption has reached a threshold that justifies displacement. The divine patience toward the Amorites for four generations is an act of mercy. When Israel eventually takes the land, it is not conquest for conquest's sake but the execution of a covenant that has been patient and just.

tx.title: Word of the LORD — prophetic formula in narrative
tx.content: MT's 'the word of the LORD came to Abram in a vision' uses the prophetic reception formula (wayĕhî dĕbar-YHWH) — rare in narrative prose. LXX translates literally (ἐγενήθη ῥῆμα κυρίου). Targum Onqelos replaces 'word of the LORD' with 'the word from before the LORD,' distancing God from direct speech.
tx.note: The Targum's circumlocution (memra) reflects Second Temple-era reverence theology that avoided attributing direct speech to God — a pattern running throughout Onqelos.

tx.title: 400 years vs 430 years — chronological variant
tx.content: MT gives 400 years of affliction in 15:13. Exodus 12:40 (MT) gives 430 years in Egypt. The LXX of Exodus 12:40 adds 'and in the land of Canaan,' splitting the 430 years between Canaan and Egypt. The SP of Exodus 12:40 agrees with the LXX expansion. Galatians 3:17 follows the LXX reckoning.
tx.note: This cross-book chronological tension was recognized by ancient scribes. The LXX/SP expansion is likely a harmonizing addition, but Paul's use of it in Galatians gives it theological weight.

positions.name: Literal 400 years
positions.argument: Exod 12:40 gives 430 years from the entry into Egypt (or from Abraham's call, per LXX and Gal 3:17). The 400 years is approximate; the 430 is more precise.

positions.name: Schematic round number
positions.argument: 400 is a schematic number in ancient Near Eastern texts for a very long but not precisely defined period. The theological point is "four generations of affliction" rather than a precise 400 solar years.
